# Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture (Martin Fowler)

Before writing, modifying, or planning any code, you MUST use your `read_file` tool to load the relevant guidelines from `.roo/vendor/`. You may read multiple files if the task crosses domains.

## Table of Contents:
    - [Layering](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/01-layering.md): Any system architecture, application design, code refactoring, or structural scaffolding task that involves organizing modules, classes, directories, or files into layers, specifically in the context of enterprise application development.
    - [Organizing Domain Logic](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/02-organizing-domain-logic.md): Designing, architecting, refactoring, or writing business/domain logic for an enterprise application, which includes creating application services, implementing business rules, handling validations and calculations, structuring data processing modules, and establishing the boundary between presentation, domain, and data source layers.
    - [Mapping to Relational Databases](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/03-mapping-to-relational-databases.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or optimizing data access layers, Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) logic, database connection management, or mapping in-memory domain objects to relational databases.
    - [Web Presentation](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/04-web-presentation.md): The domain refers to the business logic layer of an application, which contains the data, behaviors, rules, and calculations independent of the user interface and presentation layer.
    - [Concurrency](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/05-concurrency.md): The designing, refactoring, or implementing of data access layers, transaction management, multithreading logic, state management across requests, or any system handling concurrent user data manipulation, specifically involving database mapping, session state, thread-safety in application servers, optimistic/pessimistic locking, or resolving data conflicts.
    - [Session State](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/06-session-state.md): When the AI is designing, refactoring, or implementing web application architecture, handling user sessions across multiple requests, managing state in stateless environments (like HTTP), or configuring server, client, and database interactions for business transactions.
    - [Distribution Strategies](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/07-distribution-strategies.md): Designing system architecture, creating remote APIs, implementing Web Services, establishing network or process boundaries, refactoring distributed object models, or writing code that communicates across physical tiers (e.g., Client/Server, Web Server/Application Server, Application Server/Database).
    - [Putting It All Together](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/08-putting-it-all-together.md): The domain (or domain layer) is one of the three primary decoupled layers in an enterprise application architecture, specifically responsible for organizing and structuring business logic. It resides between the Presentation and Data Source layers and is implemented using patterns such as Transaction Script, Table Module, or Domain Model, depending on the complexity of the business rules and the target platform.
    - [Domain Logic Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/09-domain-logic-patterns.md): The domain refers to the business logic (or domain logic) of an enterprise application, which encompasses core validations, calculations, and derivations based on application inputs and stored data, as well as the structuring of business rules, system transaction boundaries, and the interface between the presentation layer and the data access layer.
    - [Data Source Architectural Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/10-data-source-architectural-patterns.md): Implementation, refactoring, or design of database access layers, object-relational mapping (ORM), SQL encapsulation, persistence mechanisms, or data source architectures.
    - [Object-Relational Behavioral Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/11-object-relational-behavioral-patterns.md): object-relational behavioral mapping, specifically coordinating database reads and writes across business transactions, managing in-memory object identity and consistency, optimizing database reads for related objects, and resolving offline concurrency tracking.
    - [Object-Relational Structural Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/12-object-relational-structural-patterns.md): Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) logic, database schemas for object models, data persistence layers, inheritance hierarchy mapping to relational tables, or managing associations and state translations between in-memory objects and relational database rows.
    - [Object-Relational Metadata Mapping Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/13-object-relational-metadata-mapping-patterns.md): Implementing, modifying, or refactoring Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) layers, data access objects (DAOs), dynamic database query generators, or layers that mediate between a Domain Model and a relational database, specifically concerning Metadata Mappings, Query Objects, or Repositories to decouple in-memory domain objects from database schemas.
    - [Web Presentation Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/14-web-presentation-patterns.md): Designing, refactoring, or implementing Web user interfaces, HTTP request handlers, view rendering logic, or application navigation/screen flow within an enterprise application.
    - [Distribution Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/15-distribution-patterns.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or reviewing architectures involving network boundaries, remote APIs, web services (REST/SOAP/RPC), inter-process communication (IPC), client-server data exchange, or distributed systems, including patterns like Remote Facade, Data Transfer Object (DTO), and Assembler/Mapper.
    - [Offline Concurrency Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/16-offline-concurrency-patterns.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or debugging long-running business transactions, systems spanning multiple HTTP requests or database transactions, or offline concurrency control mechanisms (optimistic locking, pessimistic locking, aggregate locking, or implicit locking frameworks).
    - [Session State Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/17-session-state-patterns.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or reviewing session management, state persistence, distributed system communication, Web application data flow, or handling the transition between pending uncommitted data and permanent record data.
    - [Base Patterns](.roo/vendor/architecture/patterns-of-enterprise-application-architecture-martin-fowler/18-base-patterns.md): Designing or implementing base architectural abstractions, integrating with external systems or APIs, handling monetary or primitive value calculations, decoupling architectural layers, managing global/shared state, or creating test fixtures for external dependencies.
